one_line_takeaway: EnsembleCert combines a polynomial-time IP-to-MCKP reduction with ScaLabelCert's polynomial-time exact NTK-based certificate to give white-box, tighter certified robustness against label-flipping poisoning for partition-aggregation ensembles, showing on CIFAR-10 that 10 partitions with white-box knowledge can certify 26.5% more median label flips than 1000 black-box partitions -- undermining the assumption that heavy partitioning is required for strong poisoning certificates.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10, EnsembleCert (white-box) can certify up to 26.5% more label flips in median (MCR) than SS-DPA (black-box) while using 100x fewer partitions (e.g., 10 vs 1000 partitions) -- the headline result illustrated in Fig. 1b. More generally, across CIFAR-10, MNIST, and MNIST 1-vs-7 with kernel-SVM base classifiers, the white-box MCR is largely invariant to the number of partitions until a convergence point, after which it matches the black-box bound exactly (the white-box and black-box certificates provably coincide once the black-box worst-case assumption becomes tight). With kernel-ridge-regression base classifiers, certified robustness is regularization-dependent and can be non-monotonic in the number of partitions: on CIFAR-10 with lambda=100, EnsembleCert certifies a median of 219 label flips with 10 partitions versus only 173 with 1000 partitions; on MNIST with lambda=0.1, it certifies 356 flips with 12 partitions versus 82 flips with 1200 partitions -- i.e., more partitioning can strictly hurt certified robustness when the base classifier is already highly robust (high lambda). In the no-partitioning (Np=1) regime enabled by ScaLabelCert's polynomial-time exact certificate, a single kernel-SVM model on CIFAR-10 achieves non-trivial certified accuracy up to 1000 label flips (2% of training data), whereas the SS-DPA evaluation in Levine & Feizi (2021) fails to certify any test sample beyond 500 flips; standalone models also outperform their partition-aggregated ensemble counterparts across the tested settings (Fig. 3), including a case on MNIST 1-vs-7 where a standalone kernel-regression model retains ~80% certified accuracy at a budget where the best-performing ensemble (Np=10) has already dropped to 0%. Collectively these results challenge the prevailing assumption that heavy partitioning is necessary (or even helpful) for strong certified robustness against label-flipping poisoning.
core_contribution: The paper introduces two coupled contributions. (1) EnsembleCert: the first white-box certification framework for partition-aggregation ensembles (e.g., SS-DPA) against label-flipping poisoning attacks. Instead of treating base classifiers as black boxes (worst-case assumption: any single label flip in a partition can flip that base classifier's vote), EnsembleCert uses per-partition white-box knowledge rho^c_i (the minimum number of label flips in partition i needed to change base classifier f_i's prediction to class c) and aggregates it into an ensemble-level certificate. The aggregation is posed as an Integer Program (P1) that is NP-hard in the worst case (O(K x 2^(Np x K))); the authors prove (Theorem 1) that P1 is equivalent to a relaxed problem P2, which they show reduces to a Multiple Choice Knapsack Problem (MCKP) solvable via dynamic programming in O(Np^2) per class, making the full ensemble certificate polynomial-time. (2) ScaLabelCert: a method to efficiently generate the white-box per-classifier knowledge that EnsembleCert consumes. It builds on LabelCert (Sabanayagam et al., 2025, ICLR) -- which gives an exact but NP-hard/MILP-based certificate for infinite-width NNs against label flipping via the NN-SVM equivalence under the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) -- and shows (Theorem 2) that for a sufficiently small SVM regularization constant C, the dual variables alpha become label-invariant (alpha = C * 1_n), which collapses the certificate computation to a single-level combinatorial problem solvable in polynomial time (binary case) or via provably tight polynomial-time upper/lower bounds (multi-class, one-vs-all case, since the exact multi-class problem is itself NP-hard). ScaLabelCert also extends the formulation from kernel-SVM (soft-margin) base classifiers to kernel-ridge-regression base classifiers via the NTK equivalence for regression losses. The combination yields the first exact, polynomial-time certificate for (infinite-width) neural networks against label-flipping poisoning that scales to CIFAR-10/MNIST-sized image benchmarks.
threat_model: Label-flipping data poisoning: an adversary can change up to r of the n training labels (an ℓ2/ℓ0-ball constraint on the label vector, ||y_tilde - y||_0 <= r) before training; features/inputs are not perturbed and the adversary has no control over the unlabeled data used for partitioning in SS-DPA (partitioning is done on sorted unlabeled features, making it label-flip-invariant). The certification goal is the certified radius r_tilde(t): the maximum r up to which the model's (or ensemble's) prediction on a fixed test point t is provably unchanged for every possible r-label-flip perturbation of the training set. A certificate is 'exact' if it equals the true certified radius rather than a conservative lower bound. This is a training-time integrity / poisoning-robustness threat model, not an inference-time evasion or extraction threat model.
limitations: (1) ScaLabelCert's exactness relies on the infinite-width NN <-> kernel equivalence, which is only exact in the infinite-width limit; for finite width w, the output gap to the SVM/kernel-regression prediction is O(ln(w)/sqrt(w)) with high probability (per Gosch et al. 2025; Liu et al. 2020), so certificates for real (finite-width) networks are asymptotic/approximate, not exact. (2) The kernel-SVM simplification (Theorem 2) requires a sufficiently small regularization constant C (bounded by 1/max_i sum_j |Q^j_i|), which trades off clean accuracy for the ability to eliminate the inner optimization problem; the authors note this may not be optimal for clean accuracy. (3) The multi-class ScaLabelCert certificate (O1(c')) is itself NP-hard to solve exactly; the paper only provides polynomial-time-computable tight upper and lower bounds for it, not the exact value, in the multi-class setting. (4) The primary/best-performing instantiation (ScaLabelCert on infinite-width NNs) is restricted to relatively simple architectures (one conv layer without pooling; one FC hidden layer without non-linearity) and depends on pretrained self-supervised feature extractors (RotNet, SimCLR) from prior work rather than end-to-end learned representations. (5) Kernel-regression-based ScaLabelCert without partitioning is computationally expensive (requires the full training kernel), so the no-partition evaluation with kernel regression is limited to the small binary MNIST 1-vs-7 dataset rather than CIFAR-10/MNIST at scale. (6) The paper is an ICLR 2026 Workshop submission (not yet a full conference paper), and the finite-width-NN and smoothed-linear-classifier instantiations of EnsembleCert are relegated to appendices with less extensive evaluation than the primary infinite-width results.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10: EnsembleCert certifies up to 26.5% more label flips in median (MCR) than SS-DPA while using 100x fewer partitions (e.g., 10 vs 1000 partitions)
CIFAR-10, lambda=100 (kernel ridge regression base classifiers): EnsembleCert certifies median 219 label flips with 10 partitions vs 173 label flips with 1000 partitions
MNIST, lambda=0.1 (kernel ridge regression base classifiers): EnsembleCert certifies 356 label flips with 12 partitions vs 82 label flips with 1200 partitions

CIFAR-10, no partitioning (Np=1), kernel SVM via ScaLabelCert: non-trivial certified accuracy up to 1000 label flips (2% of training data), versus SS-DPA (Levine & Feizi 2021) which fails to certify any test sample beyond 500 label flips
MNIST 1-vs-7: standalone kernel-regression model maintains certified accuracy near 80% at a budget where the best-performing ensemble (Np=10) reaches 0% certified accuracy
IP-to-MCKP reduction lowers ensemble-certificate complexity from O(K x 2^(Np x K)) (naive ILP) to O(Np^2) per class via dynamic programming
baselines_compared: SS-DPA / Semi-Supervised Deep Partition Aggregation (Levine & Feizi, 2021) -- black-box partition-aggregation certificate, primary baseline
LabelCert (Sabanayagam et al., 2025) -- exact but NP-hard/MILP white-box certificate for NNs against label poisoning that ScaLabelCert builds on and scales up

Gradient-based parameter-bounding certificate (Sosnin et al., 2025) -- alternative white-box certificate for finite-width NNs, compared against ScaLabelCert on CIFAR-10 (appendix Sec. D.3) and also used as an alternate base-classifier certifier within EnsembleCert (appendix Sec. D.4)

Randomized smoothing certificate (Rosenfeld et al., 2020) -- used both as a point of comparison and as an alternative certification method for smoothed-linear-classifier base classifiers within EnsembleCert
